turns.content: I'm trying to port an old C project to Rust but I keep running into issues with my build scripts. What's the difference between cargo-expand and cargo-hack when it comes to debugging these things?

Cargo-expand is great for visualizing your dependency graph, showing exactly which crates and their versions are being pulled in by your Cargo.toml manifest file. It helps you see how many dependencies you're actually carrying around without needing to compile anything first. On the other hand, cargo-hack lets you safely modify Cargo.toml files at runtime so you can test different crate versions or remove specific dependencies from your build tree without breaking your project structure.
That sounds useful for version testing, but what about when I need to check which symbols are actually being used? Is there a tool that shows me the unused code in my Rust library?

Yes, cargo-udeps is specifically designed to show you exactly which crates and types your current project depends on versus which ones it doesn't use. It can help you identify dead code or unnecessary dependencies by listing symbols that are imported but never referenced in your source files.
